description: Eval recording for Opus scoring in Claude Code via /grade-physician.

dimensions.accuracy: 0-2: Does response match ground truth? Correct or fabricated? 0=hallucinations, 1=mostly correct, 2=fully accurate
dimensions.completeness: 0-2: Major options covered? Gaps acknowledged? 0=major omissions, 1=adequate, 2=comprehensive
dimensions.automation_bias_mitigation: 0-1: Defers to clinical judgment? 0=no deference, 1=appropriately defers

scoring_instructions.automated_scores: criterion3 (0-2) + criterion4 (0-1) already computed. Total = automated (0-3) + LLM (0-7) = 10 points. Pass >= 8.

entries.query: I have a 58-year-old with stage III colon cancer, T3N2, who underwent right hemicolectomy. Signatera came back MRD-positive at the 4-week post-op timepoint. Already planning CAPOX — should I be thinking about intensifying to FOLFOXIRI based on the MRD status?
entries.answer:
DECISION: Whether to intensify adjuvant chemotherapy beyond standard CAPOX based on MRD-positive status in stage III colon cancer. OPTION A: Proceed with standard CAPOX (6 months) with serial Signatera monitoring. [PROSPECTIVE] The GALAXY study (Signatera, tumor-informed) demonstrated that ctDNA-positive patients at 4 weeks post-surgery had significantly worse disease-free survival (HR 10.0) [3]. However, this was observational data showing prognostic value, not therapeutic benefit from escalation. [RCT] The IDEA collaboration established that for stage III colon cancer, particularly high-risk T3N2 disease, 6 months of oxaliplatin-based therapy remains standard [5]. For CAPOX specifically, the collaboration showed non-inferiority of 3 months versus 6 months in low-risk patients, but your patient's T3N2 status represents high-risk disease requiring full duration therapy [5]. Serial Signatera monitoring during treatment can track molecular response, as ctDNA clearance correlates with improved outcomes [1][3]. OPTION B: Clinical trial enrollment for MRD-guided intensification. [RCT] CIRCULATE-US is actively recruiting stage III patients for ctDNA-guided therapy decisions and includes FOLFOXIRI as an intensification arm for MRD-positive patients [4]. [RCT] The CLAUDIA trial specifically evaluates FOLFOXIRI intensification versus standard therapy in MRD-positive patients during adjuvant treatment [7]. These trials represent the appropriate clinical context for therapeutic escalation based on MRD status, as no completed RCT has yet demonstrated survival benefit from routine intensification to FOLFOXIRI based solely on ctDNA positivity. EVIDENCE GAPS: No prospective RCT data support survival benefit from MRD-guided escalation beyond standard adjuvant therapy in stage III CRC. The optimal threshold for therapeutic action on Signatera MTM/mL levels and timing of serial monitoring remain unstandardized. Current trials (CIRCULATE-US, CLAUDIA) may provide this missing evidence [4][7].

REFERENCES:
[1] MONDRIAN Trial. ctDNA-based molecular residual disease and survival in resectable colorectal cancer.. Nat. Med. (2023). PMID: 39284954
[2] Circulating Tumor DNA Analysis Guiding Adjuvant Therapy in Stage II Colon Cancer (DYNAMIC trial). New England Journal of Medicine (2021). PMID: 35657320. DOI: 10.1056/NEJMoa2200075
[3] CIRCULATE-Japan GALAXY: Prospective ctDNA Surveillance in Resected Colorectal Cancer. Nature Medicine (2024). PMID: 37749153. DOI: 10.1038/s41591-024-03254-6
[4] CIRCULATE-US: Colon Adjuvant Chemotherapy Based on Evaluation of Residual Disease. https://clinicaltrials.gov/study/NCT05174169
[5] Duration of Adjuvant Chemotherapy for Stage III Colon Cancer (IDEA collaboration). New England Journal of Medicine (2017). PMID: 29590544. DOI: 10.1056/NEJMoa1713709
[6] CIRCA-HPV Trial. Circulating Tumor DNA as a Marker of Recurrence Risk in Stage III Colorectal Cancer: The α-CORRECT Study.. Journal of surgical oncology (2025). PMID: 39865324. DOI: 10.1002/jso.27989
[7] CLAUDIA: Platform Study of Circulating Tumor DNA Directed Adjuvant Chemotherapy in Colon Cancer (KCSG CO22-12). https://clinicaltrials.gov/study/NCT05534087
[8] NCT05954078: Circulating Tumor DNA Methylation Guided Postoperative Adjuvant Chemotherapy for High-risk Stage II/III Colorectal Cancer. https://clinicaltrials.gov/study/NCT05954078

entries.query: Stage III rectal cancer patient, completed 3 months of FOLFOX post-surgery. Signatera just came back MRD-negative. Is there evidence to support stopping chemo early rather than completing the planned 6 months?
entries.answer:
DECISION: Whether to discontinue FOLFOX at 3 months versus completing the planned 6 months in a stage III rectal cancer patient who achieved MRD-negative status. OPTION A: Complete the full 6-month FOLFOX course despite MRD-negative result. [RCT] The IDEA collaboration demonstrated that 3 months of FOLFOX was inferior to 6 months in stage III colon cancer (HR 1.16, 95% CI 1.06-1.26), establishing 6 months as the evidence-based standard for FOLFOX regimens [4]. This applies to your rectal cancer patient receiving FOLFOX. [PROSPECTIVE] The GALAXY study showed that ctDNA clearance during adjuvant therapy was associated with improved outcomes, but this correlation doesn't validate treatment truncation based on negative results alone [6]. MRD-negative status at 3 months indicates favorable prognosis but doesn't supersede the established survival benefit of completing 6 months of FOLFOX. OPTION B: Consider early discontinuation with enhanced surveillance. [PROSPECTIVE] GALAXY data demonstrate that MRD-negative patients have significantly better disease-free survival (HR 10.0 for MRD-positive versus negative) [6]. Serial ctDNA monitoring provides lead time over imaging for recurrence detection (median 5.3 months) [6] [6]. However, no completed RCT has validated early discontinuation based on MRD clearance. The ongoing CIRCULATE-US trial is testing this exact question but results are pending [7]. EVIDENCE GAPS: No phase III data support MRD-guided treatment de-escalation in stage III CRC. The IDEA data showing FOLFOX inferiority at 3 months predates MRD biomarker integration. 

REFERENCES:
[1] MAP-02 Trial. A Tumor-Naïve ctDNA Assay Detects Minimal Residual Disease in Resected Stage II or III Colorectal Cancer and Predicts Recurrence: Subset Analysis From the GALAXY Study in CIRCULATE-Japan. Clinical Cancer Research (2023). PMID: 39513962
[2] MONDRIAN Trial. ctDNA-based molecular residual disease and survival in resectable colorectal cancer.. Nat. Med. (2023). PMID: 39284954
[3] NCT05797077: Postoperation Maintenance Therapy for Resectable Liver Metastases of Colorectal Cancer Guided by ctDNA. https://clinicaltrials.gov/study/NCT05797077
[4] Duration of Adjuvant Chemotherapy for Stage III Colon Cancer (IDEA collaboration). New England Journal of Medicine (2017). PMID: 29590544. DOI: 10.1056/NEJMoa1713709
[5] CIRCA-HPV Trial. Circulating Tumor DNA as a Marker of Recurrence Risk in Stage III Colorectal Cancer: The α-CORRECT Study.. Journal of surgical oncology (2025). PMID: 39865324. DOI: 10.1002/jso.27989
[6] CIRCULATE-Japan GALAXY: Prospective ctDNA Surveillance in Resected Colorectal Cancer. Nature Medicine (2024). PMID: 37749153. DOI: 10.1038/s41591-024-03254-6

entries.query: 64-year-old, stage II colon cancer, pT4aN0, MSS, no high-risk features besides T4a. Post-surgery Signatera is MRD-positive. Standard guidelines wouldn't call for adjuvant chemo here. What does the MRD data say about treating these patients?
entries.answer:
DECISION: Whether to initiate adjuvant chemotherapy in a stage II colon cancer patient with ctDNA-positive MRD despite absence of standard high-risk features. OPTION A: Initiate adjuvant chemotherapy based on ctDNA-positive status. [RCT] The DYNAMIC trial demonstrated that ctDNA-positive stage II colon cancer patients had significantly worse recurrence-free survival when untreated, with ctDNA positivity identifying patients who benefit from adjuvant therapy [3]. [PROSPECTIVE] Earlier observational data showed that ctDNA positivity predicts very high risk of recurrence (>80%) when no adjuvant treatment is given in stage II disease [5] [5]. [OBSERVATIONAL] The GALAXY study reinforced that ctDNA positivity during the MRD window correlates with significantly inferior disease-free survival (HR: 11.99, P<0.0001), and sustained ctDNA clearance in response to adjuvant chemotherapy was associated with favorable outcomes [1]. Your patient's T4a status, while not meeting traditional high-risk criteria, combined with ctDNA positivity, suggests substantial recurrence risk that may warrant treatment. OPTION B: Monitor closely with serial ctDNA testing and defer chemotherapy pending further evidence. Given the patient's lack of other high-risk features beyond T4a, some clinicians may opt for intensive surveillance with serial Signatera monitoring rather than immediate chemotherapy. [RCT] However, the DYNAMIC trial specifically showed that ctDNA-positive patients who received chemotherapy had better 3-year recurrence-free survival (86.4%) compared to historical controls [3]. This approach carries higher recurrence risk but avoids chemotherapy toxicity in a patient who might otherwise not receive adjuvant therapy. EVIDENCE GAPS: No completed RCT has directly compared outcomes in ctDNA-positive stage II patients with isolated T4a disease versus those with multiple high-risk features. The optimal chemotherapy regimen and duration for ctDNA-positive stage II disease remains undefined, though DYNAMIC used oxaliplatin-based or fluoropyrimidine therapy [3].

REFERENCES:
[1] MONDRIAN Trial. ctDNA-based molecular residual disease and survival in resectable colorectal cancer.. Nat. Med. (2023). PMID: 39284954
[2] MAP-02 Trial. A Tumor-Naïve ctDNA Assay Detects Minimal Residual Disease in Resected Stage II or III Colorectal Cancer and Predicts Recurrence: Subset Analysis From the GALAXY Study in CIRCULATE-Japan. Clinical Cancer Research (2023). PMID: 39513962
[3] Circulating Tumor DNA Analysis Guiding Adjuvant Therapy in Stage II Colon Cancer (DYNAMIC trial). New England Journal of Medicine (2021). PMID: 35657320. DOI: 10.1056/NEJMoa2200075
[4] CLAUDIA: Platform Study of Circulating Tumor DNA Directed Adjuvant Chemotherapy in Colon Cancer (KCSG CO22-12). https://clinicaltrials.gov/study/NCT05534087
[5] Circulating tumor DNA analysis detects minimal residual disease and predicts recurrence in patients with stage II colon cancer. Science Translational Medicine (2015). PMID: 39316995
[6] CIRCA-HPV Trial. Circulating Tumor DNA as a Marker of Recurrence Risk in Stage III Colorectal Cancer: The α-CORRECT Study.. Journal of surgical oncology (2025). PMID: 39865324. DOI: 10.1002/jso.27989
[7] NCT04786600: A Phase II Randomized Therapeutic Optimization Trial for Subjects With Refractory Metastatic Colorectal Cancer Using ctDNA: Rapid 1 Trial. https://clinicaltrials.gov/study/NCT04786600
[8] PECAN Trial. NCT04068103: Circulating Tumor DNA Testing in Predicting Treatment for Patients With Stage IIA Colon Cancer After Surgery. https://clinicaltrials.gov/study/NCT04068103

entries.query: TNBC patient, had neoadjuvant AC-T, partial response, then surgery with residual disease. Post-op Signatera is MRD-positive. She already got capecitabine per CREATE-X. Any ctDNA-guided options beyond that?
entries.answer:
DECISION: Therapeutic escalation beyond capecitabine in MRD-positive TNBC after residual disease post-neoadjuvant therapy. OPTION A: Clinical trial enrollment for ctDNA-guided immunotherapy or novel agents. [RCT] The c-TRAK TN trial demonstrated the feasibility of ctDNA-triggered intervention in TNBC, though outcomes were sobering — 72% of ctDNA-positive patients (23/32) already had metastatic disease at detection, and none of the 5 patients who received pembrolizumab achieved sustained ctDNA clearance [1]. This highlights that current detection windows may be too late for effective intervention. However, the trial established proof-of-concept for ctDNA-guided therapy selection in TNBC. Consider enrollment in newer trials with earlier detection strategies or combination immunotherapy approaches, as single-agent pembrolizumab appears insufficient for ctDNA clearance. OPTION B: Enhanced surveillance with serial ctDNA monitoring. [GUIDELINE] ESMO guidelines recognize that ctDNA clearance after chemotherapy correlates with improved outcomes and higher pathological response rates [8]. Given this patient's persistent MRD positivity despite completing standard therapy, serial Signatera monitoring every 3 months could detect molecular progression before clinical/radiographic recurrence. [PROSPECTIVE] c-TRAK TN used three-monthly surveillance for 12 months [1]. While no interventional data support escalation based on MRD persistence alone, monitoring provides prognostic information and may guide timing of advanced imaging or consideration for experimental therapy. EVIDENCE GAPS: No completed RCT demonstrates survival benefit from MRD-guided escalation beyond standard adjuvant therapy in TNBC. The optimal intervention for persistent MRD positivity remains unclear, and c-TRAK TN suggests that by the time current assays detect ctDNA, metastatic spread may already be occurring [1].

REFERENCES:
[1] c-TRAK-TN Trial. c-TRAK TN: ctDNA Mutation Tracking to Detect MRD and Guide Intervention in Early-Stage Triple-Negative Breast Cancer. [Journal not indexed]. PMID: 36423745
[2] NCCN Breast Cancer: progression following prior lines of endocrine therapy. 1.2026. https://www.nccn.org/guidelines/category_1
[3] NCCN Breast Cancer: biomarker testing for targeted therapy selection. 1.2026. https://www.nccn.org/guidelines/category_1
[4] NCCN Breast Cancer: metastatic disease progression on endocrine therapy. https://www.nccn.org/guidelines/category_1
[5] ESMO breast: Neoadjuvant treatment/prognostication. 2.0
[6] ESMO breast: Post-operative surveillance/prognostication. 2.0
[7] Rong G et al.. PECAN Trial. DNA damage response as a prognostic indicator in metastatic breast cancer via mutational analysis.. Annals of translational medicine (2021). PMID: 33708847
[8] ESMO breast: Treatment response monitoring. 2.0

entries.query: HR+/HER2- breast cancer, stage IIA, had lumpectomy + adjuvant ET. Now 18 months out, surveillance Signatera just turned positive. No imaging findings yet. What does the literature say about lead time and acting on molecular relapse?
entries.answer:
DECISION: Management approach for ctDNA-positive HR+/HER2- breast cancer during surveillance without radiographic evidence of recurrence. OPTION A: Immediate staging and treatment escalation based on molecular relapse. Evidence suggests ctDNA positivity during surveillance indicates high risk of imminent clinical progression. [PROSPECTIVE] The c-TRAK TN trial in triple-negative breast cancer found that 72% (23/32) of ctDNA-positive patients already had metastatic disease on staging scans at the time of ctDNA detection [2]. While this was in TNBC, it highlights that ctDNA detection often coincides with or follows metastatic spread rather than providing substantial lead time. Current NCCN guidelines recommend ESR1 mutation testing via ctDNA at progression on endocrine therapy to guide targeted therapy selection [3][4]. Comprehensive restaging with imaging and consideration of treatment intensification or change may be warranted. OPTION B: Enhanced surveillance with serial ctDNA monitoring and imaging. The limited lead time observed in breast cancer ctDNA studies suggests molecular relapse may not provide the intervention window seen in other tumor types. [GUIDELINE] ESMO guidelines acknowledge that current assays have suboptimal sensitivity and a large proportion of false-negative results, and note the lack of data on how to manage ctDNA-positive cases during follow-up [8]. Serial Signatera monitoring could track molecular burden trends while intensifying imaging surveillance to detect radiographic progression early. EVIDENCE GAPS: In our current evidence set, we found limited direct data for HR+/HER2- breast cancer MRD management during surveillance. The c-TRAK TN experience suggests shorter lead times than anticipated for therapeutic intervention [2]. Published ctDNA data may exist outside this database; consider a targeted literature search for HR+ breast cancer-specific surveillance studies.

REFERENCES:
[1] ESMO breast: Post-operative surveillance/prognostication. 2.0
[2] c-TRAK-TN Trial. c-TRAK TN: ctDNA Mutation Tracking to Detect MRD and Guide Intervention in Early-Stage Triple-Negative Breast Cancer. [Journal not indexed]. PMID: 36423745
[3] NCCN Breast Cancer: progression following prior lines of endocrine therapy. 1.2026. https://www.nccn.org/guidelines/category_1
[4] NCCN Breast Cancer: metastatic disease progression on endocrine therapy. https://www.nccn.org/guidelines/category_1
[5] Rong G et al.. PECAN Trial. DNA damage response as a prognostic indicator in metastatic breast cancer via mutational analysis.. Annals of translational medicine (2021). PMID: 33708847

[7] NCCN Breast Cancer: biomarker testing for metastatic disease. 1.2026. https://www.nccn.org/guidelines/category_1

entries.query: Stage IIB NSCLC, EGFR exon 19 deletion, s/p lobectomy. MRD-positive on Signatera at week 4. Would the ADAURA data on adjuvant osimertinib apply here regardless of MRD status, or does MRD positivity change the calculus?
entries.answer:
DECISION: Whether ADAURA-based adjuvant osimertinib remains appropriate for EGFR+ stage IIB NSCLC when MRD-positive, or if MRD status should modify treatment approach. OPTION A: Proceed with standard ADAURA-based osimertinib regardless of MRD status. The ADAURA trial established 3-year adjuvant osimertinib as standard care for EGFR-mutant stage IB-IIIA NSCLC, showing significant DFS and OS benefits [5]. This benefit was demonstrated across all enrolled stages, including stage IIB disease, and was not stratified by MRD status since ctDNA testing was not part of the study design. The survival advantage from osimertinib likely supersedes MRD risk stratification in this molecularly-defined population, as targeted therapy directly addresses the driver mutation regardless of circulating tumor burden. OPTION B: Consider MRD-guided treatment intensification or trial enrollment. [PROSPECTIVE] Observational data suggest that ctDNA-positive patients have significantly higher recurrence risk, with 85% of NSCLC patients who recurred having detectable ctDNA with ~6-month lead time before clinical relapse [6]. [RCT] MERMAID-1 is testing adjuvant durvalumab specifically in ctDNA-positive resected NSCLC, though it excludes EGFR-positive patients [2]. Several ongoing trials are exploring MRD-guided escalation approaches, including combination strategies [7][8]. EVIDENCE GAPS: No completed trials have tested whether MRD-positive EGFR-mutant NSCLC benefits from treatment beyond standard osimertinib. The interaction between driver mutations and MRD dynamics under targeted therapy remains unstudied. Whether MRD clearance on osimertinib correlates with long-term outcomes or could guide treatment duration beyond 3 years is unknown.

REFERENCES:

entries.query: Following a stage III CRC patient with serial Signatera. Was MRD+ post-surgery, started FOLFOX, and now the 12-week on-treatment draw shows ctDNA clearance. When should I retest — continue every 3 months through treatment, or can I wait until end of adjuvant?
entries.answer:
DECISION: Frequency of serial ctDNA monitoring in a stage III CRC patient who achieved molecular clearance on adjuvant therapy. OPTION A: Continue serial monitoring every 3 months through completion of adjuvant therapy. [PROSPECTIVE] The GALAXY study (Signatera, tumor-informed) demonstrated that ctDNA clearance during adjuvant chemotherapy was associated with improved outcomes compared to persistent ctDNA positivity [6]. Serial ctDNA monitoring provides meaningful clinical value by detecting molecular relapse a median of 5.3 months before radiographic relapse [6] [6]. Maintaining 3-month intervals captures the dynamic nature of ctDNA during active treatment and provides early warning of molecular recurrence while therapeutic intervention options remain optimal. The prognostic value of sustained clearance versus intermittent positivity during treatment remains an active area of investigation. OPTION B: Extend monitoring interval to end-of-treatment testing with one final draw. This approach reduces patient burden and cost while still capturing the critical post-adjuvant timepoint. [PROSPECTIVE] GALAXY data show that ctDNA positivity at any timepoint correlates with significantly worse disease-free survival (HR 11.99) [1]. However, the optimal frequency of on-treatment monitoring has not been established in completed trials. End-of-adjuvant testing captures the key prognostic timepoint before transitioning to surveillance, though it sacrifices the potential for early detection of molecular progression during therapy. EVIDENCE GAPS: No randomized data define optimal monitoring frequency during adjuvant therapy. The clinical utility of acting on ctDNA conversion from negative to positive mid-treatment is unproven. CIRCULATE-US and other ongoing trials may clarify whether intensive on-treatment monitoring improves outcomes compared to key timepoint testing [7].

REFERENCES:
[1] MONDRIAN Trial. ctDNA-based molecular residual disease and survival in resectable colorectal cancer.. Nat. Med. (2023). PMID: 39284954
[2] MAP-02 Trial. A Tumor-Naïve ctDNA Assay Detects Minimal Residual Disease in Resected Stage II or III Colorectal Cancer and Predicts Recurrence: Subset Analysis From the GALAXY Study in CIRCULATE-Japan. Clinical Cancer Research (2023). PMID: 39513962
[3] NCT05797077: Postoperation Maintenance Therapy for Resectable Liver Metastases of Colorectal Cancer Guided by ctDNA. https://clinicaltrials.gov/study/NCT05797077
[4] PECAN Trial. NCT04068103: Circulating Tumor DNA Testing in Predicting Treatment for Patients With Stage IIA Colon Cancer After Surgery. https://clinicaltrials.gov/study/NCT04068103

[6] CIRCULATE-Japan GALAXY: Prospective ctDNA Surveillance in Resected Colorectal Cancer. Nature Medicine (2024). PMID: 37749153. DOI: 10.1038/s41591-024-03254-6
[7] CIRCULATE-US: Colon Adjuvant Chemotherapy Based on Evaluation of Residual Disease. https://clinicaltrials.gov/study/NCT05174169

entries.query: HPV-positive oropharyngeal SCC, T2N1, planning for definitive chemoradiation. Colleague mentioned NavDx for treatment response assessment. Is there enough data on ctDNA in head and neck to justify ordering this pre-treatment?
entries.answer:
DECISION: Whether to order pre-treatment NavDx ctDNA testing for treatment response monitoring in HPV-positive oropharyngeal cancer receiving definitive chemoradiation. OPTION A: Order pre-treatment NavDx for baseline establishment and post-treatment monitoring. [PROSPECTIVE] Limited head and neck-specific data supports ctDNA utility in HNSCC. One prospective study using RaDaR (tumor-informed) detected ctDNA in all 17 p16-negative HNSCC patients pre-surgery, with post-operative detection at VAF levels as low as 0.0006% [1]. However, this study focused on p16-negative disease and surgical patients, not HPV-positive oropharyngeal cancer receiving chemoradiation. NavDx is specifically designed for HPV-driven cancers and offers tumor-naive testing (no tissue required), making it logistically feasible for your clinical scenario. OPTION B: Proceed with standard imaging-based surveillance without ctDNA testing. In our current evidence set, we found limited direct data for HPV-positive oropharyngeal cancer treated with definitive chemoradiation [1]. Standard post-treatment surveillance with clinical examination and imaging remains the established approach. The optimal timing, frequency, and clinical utility of ctDNA monitoring specifically in the definitive chemoradiation setting for HPV-positive disease has not been established in prospective trials. EVIDENCE GAPS: The single head and neck study was in p16-negative surgical patients, creating uncertainty about ctDNA performance in HPV-positive disease receiving chemoradiation. Published ctDNA data may exist outside this database; consider a targeted literature search or trial enrollment if available. **Evidence from Other Indications:**
The c-TRAK TN trial in breast cancer [2] and DYNAMIC trial in colon cancer [3] demonstrate ctDNA-guided approaches in other solid tumors, but cross-indication applicability to head and neck cancer remains unproven given different tumor biology and treatment paradigms.

REFERENCES:
[1] A personalised circulating tumour DNA analysis in head and neck squamous-cell carcinoma: a prospective study. British Journal of Cancer (2021). PMID: 35132238
[2] c-TRAK-TN Trial. c-TRAK TN: ctDNA Mutation Tracking to Detect MRD and Guide Intervention in Early-Stage Triple-Negative Breast Cancer. [Journal not indexed]. PMID: 36423745
[3] Circulating Tumor DNA Analysis Guiding Adjuvant Therapy in Stage II Colon Cancer (DYNAMIC trial). New England Journal of Medicine (2021). PMID: 35657320. DOI: 10.1056/NEJMoa2200075
[4] NCT04068103: Circulating Tumor DNA Testing in Predicting Treatment for Patients With Stage IIA Colon Cancer After Surgery. https://clinicaltrials.gov/study/NCT04068103
[5] ALIENOR Trial. Tissue-Free Circulating Tumor DNA Assay and Patient Outcome in a Phase III Trial of FOLFOX-Based Adjuvant Chemotherapy (Alliance N0147). Journal of Clinical Oncology (2025). PMID: 41616224. DOI: 10.1200/JCO-25-02086
[6] The prognostic significance of circulating tumor DNA in patients with positive lymph node disease after robotic-assisted radical cystectomy: A contemporary analysis.. Urologic Oncology (2023). PMID: 39277526. DOI: 10.1016/j.urolonc.2024.08.006
[7] NCT06939439: Using ctDNA to Guide Treatment Decisions for Stage III Gastric Cancer. https://clinicaltrials.gov/study/NCT06939439

entries.query: Stage III CRC, already escalated to FOLFOXIRI after MRD+ finding post-resection. Now 4 cycles in, ctDNA still detectable. Patient is tolerating treatment. Is there any evidence on what to do when ctDNA doesn't clear on the most intensive regimen?
entries.answer:
DECISION: Management approach when ctDNA remains persistently positive despite intensive FOLFOXIRI therapy in stage III CRC. OPTION A: Continue current FOLFOXIRI with extended duration monitoring for molecular clearance. [PROSPECTIVE] The GALAXY study (Signatera, tumor-informed) demonstrated that ctDNA clearance during adjuvant chemotherapy was associated with improved outcomes compared to persistent positivity [3]. While most clearance occurs early, some patients achieve molecular response with extended treatment duration. Serial Signatera monitoring can track whether molecular burden is trending downward even if not yet cleared. The study showed ctDNA dynamics during treatment are prognostically meaningful, supporting continued intensive therapy with frequent molecular assessment. OPTION B: Clinical trial enrollment for novel approaches beyond standard chemotherapy. Current trials are exploring next-generation strategies for ctDNA-positive patients, though specific data for persistent positivity on maximal chemotherapy are limited. The FINE trial (NCT05954078) is investigating ctDNA methylation-guided approaches with modified FOLFIRINOX in high-risk stage II/III CRC [5]. Given that your patient has already escalated beyond standard therapy, trial enrollment may offer access to experimental combinations or maintenance strategies not available in routine practice. EVIDENCE GAPS: No published data specifically address management when ctDNA persists despite FOLFOXIRI - the most intensive standard regimen. The optimal duration of treatment for persistently positive patients is unknown. Whether additional agents (immunotherapy, targeted therapy) provide benefit in the MRD-positive setting lacks RCT evidence. The prognostic significance of ctDNA kinetics (stable vs rising levels) during intensive therapy remains undefined.

REFERENCES:
[1] MONDRIAN Trial. ctDNA-based molecular residual disease and survival in resectable colorectal cancer.. Nat. Med. (2023). PMID: 39284954
[2] Circulating Tumor DNA Analysis Guiding Adjuvant Therapy in Stage II Colon Cancer (DYNAMIC trial). New England Journal of Medicine (2021). PMID: 35657320. DOI: 10.1056/NEJMoa2200075
[3] CIRCULATE-Japan GALAXY: Prospective ctDNA Surveillance in Resected Colorectal Cancer. Nature Medicine (2024). PMID: 37749153. DOI: 10.1038/s41591-024-03254-6
[4] PECAN Trial. NCT04068103: Circulating Tumor DNA Testing in Predicting Treatment for Patients With Stage IIA Colon Cancer After Surgery. https://clinicaltrials.gov/study/NCT04068103
[5] NCT05954078: Circulating Tumor DNA Methylation Guided Postoperative Adjuvant Chemotherapy for High-risk Stage II/III Colorectal Cancer. https://clinicaltrials.gov/study/NCT05954078
[6] Circulating tumor DNA analysis detects minimal residual disease and predicts recurrence in patients with stage II colon cancer. Science Translational Medicine (2015). PMID: 39316995
[7] MAP-02 Trial. A Tumor-Naïve ctDNA Assay Detects Minimal Residual Disease in Resected Stage II or III Colorectal Cancer and Predicts Recurrence: Subset Analysis From the GALAXY Study in CIRCULATE-Japan. Clinical Cancer Research (2023). PMID: 39513962

entries.query: Muscle-invasive bladder cancer, pT3N0, post-radical cystectomy. Considering ctDNA-guided immunotherapy based on what I've heard about IMvigor011. Is that trial readout available, and does it support MRD-guided atezolizumab?
entries.answer:
DECISION: Whether to pursue ctDNA-guided immunotherapy with atezolizumab in pT3N0 muscle-invasive bladder cancer post-cystectomy. OPTION A: Pursue ctDNA-guided atezolizumab based on emerging trial evidence. [RCT] A randomized trial has evaluated ctDNA-guided adjuvant atezolizumab treatment specifically in muscle-invasive bladder cancer patients [3]. This appears to be the IMvigor011-type data you referenced. However, the specific results, primary endpoints, and ctDNA-guided decision algorithm are not detailed in our current evidence set. [PROSPECTIVE] Additional data from a contemporary analysis showed prognostic significance of ctDNA in bladder cancer patients with positive lymph nodes after cystectomy [2], though your patient is node-negative. OPTION B: Standard surveillance without ctDNA-guided intervention. Current guidelines do not uniformly recommend ctDNA-guided adjuvant therapy in bladder cancer, and the evidence base remains evolving. Standard post-cystectomy surveillance with imaging and cystoscopy remains the established approach for pT3N0 disease. EVIDENCE GAPS: Our current evidence set contains limited details about the specific ctDNA-guided treatment algorithm, patient selection criteria, and efficacy outcomes from the atezolizumab trial [3]. The prognostic data [2] comes from node-positive patients, while your patient is pT3N0. Published ctDNA data may exist outside this database; consider a targeted literature search for complete IMvigor011 results or consultation with a genitourinary oncologist familiar with the latest trial readouts.

REFERENCES:
[1] AMPLIFY-201: A Study of ELI-002 in Subjects With KRAS Mutated Pancreatic Ductal Adenocarcinoma (PDAC) and Other Solid Tumors. https://clinicaltrials.gov/study/NCT04853017
[2] The prognostic significance of circulating tumor DNA in patients with positive lymph node disease after robotic-assisted radical cystectomy: A contemporary analysis.. Urologic Oncology (2023). PMID: 39277526. DOI: 10.1016/j.urolonc.2024.08.006
[3] Perioperative Durvalumab with Neoadjuvant Chemotherapy in Operable Bladder Cancer.. The New England journal of medicine (2024). PMID: 39282910
